title: Two-pointer list patterns

publication.changeSummary: Language-neutral DSA draft with explicit C/C++/Java/Python mapping for Two-pointer list patterns; independent algorithm, implementation, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Algorithmic Foundations; August 2023; material applicable to Two-pointer list patterns
sources.claim: Slow-fast pointers, sentinel nodes, and pointer-to-pointer techniques simplify list traversal, cycle detection, and edge-case updates.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Slow-fast pointers, sentinel nodes, and pointer-to-pointer techniques simplify list traversal, cycle detection, and edge-case updates.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Floyd's cycle detector advances one pointer once and another twice until they meet or the fast path ends. Your goal is to implement, prove informally and test reversal and cycle detection. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Array representation and operations; Insertion and deletion; Correctness and counterexamples. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Shared concept and invariant
sections.body: Slow-fast pointers, sentinel nodes, and pointer-to-pointer techniques simplify list traversal, cycle detection, and edge-case updates. This claim is assessed independently from syntax so conceptual evidence can transfer.

sections.heading: C, C++, Java, and Python implementation map
sections.body: In C, nodes need explicit allocation, links, and cleanup; in C++, prefer RAII ownership such as unique_ptr where it fits; in Java, node references are garbage-collected but links and equality remain explicit; in Python, object references simplify storage but do not change linear traversal complexity.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Each technique has a proof obligation and ownership implications rather than being a magic constant-time shortcut. The draft therefore rejects: Slow-fast pointers find a cycle by comparing node values rather than node identity.

includes: Use slow-fast and predecessor-current pointers for reversal, midpoint and cycle detection.
Implement, prove informally and test reversal and cycle detection.

In C, nodes need explicit allocation, links, and cleanup; in C++, prefer RAII ownership such as unique_ptr where it fits; in Java, node references are garbage-collected but links and equality remain explicit; in Python, object references simplify storage but do not change linear traversal complexity.
excludes: Each technique has a proof obligation and ownership implications rather than being a magic constant-time shortcut.

outcomes: Implement, prove informally and test reversal and cycle detection.
